# Transcrição: Lesson 35 - The Power of Culture and Social Situation to Shape Our Behavior 

- **Fonte:** Lesson 35 - The Power of Culture and Social Situation to Shape Our Behavior 
- **Duração:** 02:23
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 02:23]

We've talked about a variety of habits and behaviors and the hidden forces that shape them. But one that we haven't covered so far is also one of the most powerful. And that's the power of culture and social situation to shape our habits and choices. The cultures that we're a part of, whether they be religions or political parties, different groups of people that we organize around ideals with, people that look like us or act like us or feel like us, they influence our behavior in very profound ways. And there are a couple of examples that we can give that highlight this. You can imagine, for example, if you move to a new neighborhood and you walk out on the first Tuesday morning and all of your neighbors have their recycling bins lined out up and down the street, then you start to think, oh, I need to sign up for recycling because this is what people like us do in this neighborhood. And so those social norms drive your behavior. Similarly, a researcher named Judith Rich Harris did some very famous research on what actually influences children's behavior. In many cases, she found that the peers are actually much more powerful than the parents. What the children's friends are doing is far more powerful on what type of habits the children builds, what type of interests they have, and what they end up developing into and growing into over the course of their life. And this is true not just for children, but for us as adults as well. The peers that we have, the groups that we hang out with, the cultures that we form, they substantially influence our behavior. And one reason for this is that we often imitate the many. We imitate the people who surround us, those who are part of the groups that we join, and we replicate their behavior in some way. And so this brings us to an interesting decision point, an interesting practical take away on the power of culture and what it has on our habits and behaviors. And that is that if you want to build a new habit, you should join a culture where the normal behavior is your desired behavior, where the thing that you want to achieve is just part of the cultural norm in that situation. And when you're surrounded by people who do those things on a normal basis, you're surrounded by a whole new set of triggers and cues and rewards, right? You start to become part of that culture. When you want to make working out a habit and you have friends who are working out, it's far more easy to fall into that habit and behavior than it is if you're trying to fight against a bunch of friends who are not interested in working out or who have other interests and want to go do things differently than you do. And so by joining a culture where your desired behavior is the normal behavior, you dramatically increase the odds of success for a habit.
